Authors: Pranjal Aggarwal; Vishvak Murahari; Tanmay Rajpurohit; Ashwin Kalyan; Karthik Narasimhan; Ameet Deshpande
Venue / Status: KDD '24 / Published in Proceedings of the 30th ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining, August 25-29, 2024, Barcelona, Spain
Institution / Affiliation: Indian Institute of Technology Delhi; Princeton University; Independent researchers based in Seattle
Topic Category: Generative Engine Optimization; AI search visibility; website optimization for generative AI systems; information retrieval; search economics; creator/content visibility
Main Research Question: How can website and content creators optimize their web content to improve visibility in generative engine responses, where AI systems synthesize answers and cite sources rather than simply ranking webpages?
Key Findings: The paper formalizes Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) as a creator-centric optimization paradigm for AI-mediated search. It introduces GEO-bench, a 10K-query benchmark, and shows that GEO strategies can increase source visibility by up to about 40% in generative engine responses. The most effective strategies include adding citations, adding quotations from relevant sources, and adding statistics. Traditional keyword stuffing performs poorly, suggesting that generative engines reward credibility, evidence, and presentation quality more than simple keyword repetition. The paper also finds that GEO effectiveness varies by domain and that combined strategies can improve performance.
Data Source(s): GEO-bench: 10,000 queries from MS MARCO, ORCAS-I, Natural Questions, AllSouls, LIMA, Davinci-Debate, Perplexity.ai Discover, ELI5, and GPT-4 generated queries. Each query is augmented with cleaned text from the top 5 Google search results. The benchmark is split into 8K train, 1K validation, and 1K test queries, and includes domain/intent/difficulty-style tags.
AI Systems / Platforms Studied: GPT-3.5-turbo-based simulated generative engine; Google Search for source retrieval; Perplexity.ai as a deployed real-world generative engine. The paper also discusses systems such as BingChat, Google SGE, You.com, and Perplexity.ai as examples of generative engines.
Outcome Variables: Position-Adjusted Word Count; Word Count; citation position; Subjective Impression; relevance; influence; uniqueness; diversity; follow-up or click likelihood; subjective position; subjective count; relative improvement in visibility.
Methods: Black-box optimization framework for modifying website content. The authors apply nine GEO methods to source websites and compare source visibility before vs. after optimization. Tested methods include Authoritative, Statistics Addition, Keyword Stuffing, Cite Sources, Quotation Addition, Easy-to-Understand, Fluency Optimization, Unique Words, and Technical Terms. Experiments are averaged over five random seeds. Subjective Impression is evaluated using an LLM-based G-Eval-style procedure.
Code / GitHub Available?: Yes. The PDF states that code and data are available at the project page and mentions a public repository for prompts/method details.
Dataset Available?: Yes. The paper releases GEO-bench and states that code and data are available at the project page.
Relevance for Finance/Econ Project: Highly relevant. This paper directly supports a finance/econ project studying how firms may adapt website content and information disclosure strategies for AI-mediated search and recommendation. It can motivate research questions about digital visibility, platform intermediation, competition between firms, small-business exposure, online traffic, and changing incentives for content production under generative AI search.
Limitations / Open Gaps: The paper tests only a limited number of generative engine settings, so methods may need to adapt as generative engines evolve. GEO-bench may require updates because real-world query distributions can change over time. The study does not directly evaluate how GEO methods affect traditional search rankings. Some query tags may be noisy or subjective. Subjective Impression depends on LLM-based evaluation and may not fully capture real user attention, clicks, or economic outcomes.
Verification Status: Verified from uploaded PDF. Main claims checked against the paper text, including title/authors/venue, GEO-bench design, GEO methods, metrics, main results, Perplexity.ai experiment, and limitations.
Notes: Foundational paper for the GEO literature review. Best used as the anchor paper to define GEO, explain why traditional SEO is insufficient for generative AI search, and motivate empirical research on AI search visibility and firm/content-creator strategy.

Paper Title: SAGEO Arena: A Realistic Environment for Evaluating Search-Augmented Generative Engine Optimization
Authors: Sunghwan Kim; Wooseok Jeong; Serin Kim; Sangam Lee; Dongha Lee
Venue / Status: KDD 2026 / arXiv preprint, Work in Progress; venue listed on author page, while the arXiv version is marked Work in Progress and still contains ACM template placeholders
Topic Category: Search-Augmented Generative Engine Optimization; Generative Engine Optimization; AI search visibility; SEO; information retrieval; website optimization for generative AI systems
Paper Type: Benchmark paper + empirical evaluation + optimization framework
Main Research Question: How can web documents be optimized for visibility across the full search-augmented generative engine pipeline, including retrieval, reranking, and generation/citation, rather than only optimizing body text for citation in generated answers?
Key Findings: The paper introduces SAGEO Arena, a realistic benchmark for evaluating Search-Augmented Generative Engine Optimization across retrieval, reranking, and generation. It argues that GEO-style body-text-only optimization is insufficient in realistic AI search because rewritten content can hurt retrieval and reranking before the document reaches the generator. Structural information such as titles, meta descriptions, headings, and schema/JSON-LD plays a key role in retrieval, while body text remains important for reranking and citation. Effective AI-search visibility therefore requires stage-aware optimization tailored to each pipeline stage, not simply adding citations, statistics, or polished language to body text.
Data Source(s): SAGEO Arena corpus: 2,700 queries sampled from nine information retrieval datasets/domains, including MS MARCO/Web Search, Natural Questions/General QA, HotpotQA/Multi-hop QA, NFCorpus/Biomedical, Quora/Community QA, FiQA/Finance, DebateQA/Debate, E-commerce/Shopping, and Researchy/Academic. The authors retrieve up to 100 Google Custom Search results per query and crawl the resulting web pages, producing 171,003 unique web documents with body text and structural fields.
AI Systems / Platforms Studied: A simulated search-augmented generative engine pipeline with BM25 retrieval, cross-encoder reranking, and LLM generation with inline citations. The paper also compares optimization backbone models including GPT-5-mini, LLaMA-3.3-70B, and Qwen3-80B.
Outcome Variables: Retrieval Hit Rate; Reranking Hit Rate; Citation Rate; Rank Change / Delta Rank; citation order; stage-level visibility changes before and after optimization
Methods: The benchmark builds a full generative search pipeline: documents are crawled, structural fields and body text are extracted, passages are paired with document-level structural information, BM25 retrieval returns candidate passages, a cross-encoder reranks them, and a generator produces cited answers. For evaluation, a target document is selected from documents that reach the generation stage, optimized using a SAGEO strategy, re-indexed into the corpus, and evaluated again using the same query to measure visibility changes at retrieval, reranking, and generation.
Relevance for Finance/Econ Project: Highly relevant. This paper is directly useful for a finance/econ project on how firms may adapt website content, metadata, and disclosure structure for AI-mediated search and recommendation. It is especially relevant because it includes a Finance domain through FiQA and shows that visibility in AI search depends not only on final citation but also on earlier retrieval and reranking stages. This can motivate research questions about firm visibility, digital-market competition, AI search traffic, platform intermediation, and strategic website optimization under generative AI search.
Limitations / Open Gaps: The paper uses a constructed benchmark rather than live commercial systems such as ChatGPT, Perplexity, Gemini, or Google AI Overviews. The arXiv version is marked Work in Progress and still includes ACM template placeholders, so venue/version details should be checked later. Public SAGEO-specific code and dataset links are not currently available. The retrieval setup relies on BM25 and a modular simulated pipeline, which may not fully match proprietary AI search engines. The benchmark evaluates document-level visibility but does not directly measure user clicks, firm revenue, conversion, or real economic outcomes.
Verification Status: Partially verified: arXiv and author page checked; arXiv lists the paper as Work in Progress; author page lists KDD 2026 and code as to-be-appeared; no usable SAGEO-specific public code or dataset found yet.
Notes: Important follow-up to the original GEO paper. Best used to explain why GEO should be extended from generation-stage citation optimization to full-pipeline SAGEO. The core takeaway is that AI visibility depends on retrieval, reranking, and generation together; optimizing body text alone can reduce visibility if it harms retrieval or reranking, while structural information is central for being surfaced in the first place.

Paper Title: From Citation Selection to Citation Absorption: A Measurement Framework for Generative Engine Optimization Across AI Search Platforms
Authors: Zhang Kai; He Xinyue; Yao Jingang
Venue / Status: arXiv preprint v2 / empirical measurement framework; no peer-reviewed conference venue verified
Topic Category: Generative Engine Optimization; AI search visibility; citation behavior; citation selection; citation absorption; answer influence; AI-mediated source attribution
Paper Type: Empirical measurement framework + public dataset/pipeline paper
Main Research Question: How should GEO visibility be measured beyond simple citation counts by separating source selection from answer-level citation absorption across AI search platforms?
Key Findings: The paper argues that GEO visibility should be separated into citation selection and citation absorption. Citation breadth and citation depth diverge: Perplexity and Google cite more sources on average, while ChatGPT cites fewer sources but shows substantially higher mean influence among fetched pages. High-influence pages tend to be longer, more modular, more semantically aligned with generated answers, and richer in extractable evidence genres such as definitions, numerical facts, comparisons, how-to content, and code. A key negative finding is that Q&A formatting alone does not improve absorption, suggesting that evidence density and semantic fit matter more than surface FAQ-style formatting.
Data Source(s): Public geo-citation-lab dataset: 602 controlled prompts across ChatGPT, Google AI Overview/Gemini, and Perplexity; 21,143 valid search-layer citations; 23,745 citation-level feature records; 18,151 successfully fetched citation pages; 72 extracted features; cleaned public reports and CSV files from the GitHub repository.
Outcome Variables: Search trigger rate; citation count; citation breadth; citation selection; influence_score; citation absorption; mean/median citation influence; source type; domain type; semantic role; usage style; page structure; evidence-genre indicators; semantic alignment measures.
Methods: Descriptive cross-platform measurement study using the public geo-citation-lab dataset. The paper separates selection-stage outcomes from absorption-stage outcomes, computes platform-level citation breadth, measures page-level influence_score for fetched citations, compares source and domain types, page structure, semantic alignment, evidence genres, semantic roles, and usage styles, and proposes confirmatory models for future raw-CSV reruns. The influence_score is treated as a constructed observational proxy based on repeated references, first citation position, paragraph coverage, TF-IDF similarity, and n-gram overlap, with explicit warnings against using score components as independent predictors of the same outcome.
Relevance for Finance/Econ Project: Highly relevant. The paper provides measurable dependent variables for AI-mediated visibility beyond traditional SEO rankings and simple citation counts. Its distinction between citation selection and citation absorption can support finance/econ research on firm visibility, platform intermediation, digital-market competition, source concentration, and how firms' web disclosures or content structures may influence AI-generated answers. The dataset includes business and finance-relevant sources such as finance.yahoo.com, investopedia.com, Deloitte, WSJ, Forbes, and related institutional or news domains.
Limitations / Open Gaps: The paper is primarily descriptive and does not establish causal effects of page features on citation outcomes. The influence_score is a constructed proxy, not direct model attention or causal dependence. The prompt set is designed rather than randomly sampled from real user traffic. Absorption analysis is conditional on successfully fetched pages, so fetch failures may introduce selection bias. Platform behavior may change over time, and the study needs longitudinal replication, uncertainty estimates, confirmatory microdata analysis, and controlled content-rewrite experiments.
Verification Status: Verified: arXiv page and public GitHub repository checked; public dataset, analysis pipeline, reports, and 72-feature CSV are available in the geo-citation-lab repository.
Notes: Useful as the measurement-framework paper in the GEO literature review. It complements the original GEO paper and SAGEO Arena by distinguishing source exposure from answer-level influence. The central takeaway is that being cited is not the same as being absorbed into the generated answer; GEO should measure both citation selection and citation absorption. The evidence-container framing is especially useful for explaining why definitions, numbers/statistics, comparisons, how-to steps, code, and modular structure matter more than simply converting pages into Q&A format.

Paper Title: Think Before Writing: Feature-Level Multi-Objective Optimization for Generative Citation Visibility
Venue / Status: arXiv preprint; GitHub README states accepted to ACL 2026, but official proceedings/listing should be verified later
Institution / Affiliation: Nanjing University of Information Science and Technology
Topic Category: Generative Engine Optimization; AI search visibility; citation optimization; feature-level website optimization; multi-objective optimization; RAG-based generative search
Paper Type: Method paper + empirical evaluation + optimization framework
Main Research Question: How can webpages be optimized at the feature level, rather than through direct token-level rewriting, to improve citation visibility in generative answer engines while preserving content quality?
Key Findings: The paper proposes FeatGEO, a feature-level multi-objective optimization framework that represents webpages using interpretable structural, content, and linguistic features, then optimizes citation visibility and content quality through NSGA-II. On GEO-Bench, FeatGEO outperforms token-level GEO heuristics, AutoGEO-global, and AutoGEO-instance across GPT-4o-mini, Gemini-2.5-flash, and Qwen-plus. Reported visibility improves from 13.34 to 18.31 on GPT-4o-mini, from 8.89 to 15.35 on Gemini-2.5-flash, and from 5.20 to 10.17 on Qwen-plus, while maintaining comparable or better quality. Ablations suggest that content-level features such as statistics, cited sources, and quotations contribute strongly to citation visibility, structural features provide stable support, and language/style features have mixed context-dependent effects.
Data Source(s): GEO-Bench: 10K queries across 25 domains from nine sources, using Google Search top-5 retrieved pages plus an advertiser-controlled page injected into a controlled RAG-style generative answering pipeline.
AI Systems / Platforms Studied: Controlled generative-answer pipeline with GPT-4o-mini, Gemini-2.5-flash, and Qwen-plus as answer generators; GPT-4o-mini used as fixed page generator in the main setup; Gemini-2.5-flash and Claude-3.5-Sonnet used as alternative LLM judges for robustness.
Outcome Variables: Advertiser visibility score; word-count citation visibility; position-weighted citation visibility; LLM-judged content quality; Pareto visibility-quality trade-off; feature contribution/ablation effects.
Methods: The method first models citation behavior at the topic level by probing semantically related queries and identifying topic-consistent citation exemplars. It abstracts webpages into 13 interpretable features across structure, content, and language layers, then uses NSGA-II for black-box multi-objective feature-space optimization. Candidate feature configurations are realized into webpages through LLM generation and evaluated across topic-induced queries for both citation visibility and content quality. Experiments compare FeatGEO against baseline pages, nine token-level GEO heuristics, AutoGEO-global, and AutoGEO-instance on GEO-Bench.
Dataset Available?: Yes — uses public GEO-Bench; no new standalone dataset introduced
Relevance for Finance/Econ Project: Medium-High. The paper is strongly relevant as a method paper for understanding how firms, advertisers, or websites could optimize controllable webpage features for citation visibility in AI-generated answers. It connects naturally to finance/econ questions about firm visibility, advertising, platform intermediation, strategic content design, and competition for AI-mediated attention. However, it is less directly finance/econ than papers using real platform data or measuring actual firm/business outcomes, because it uses a controlled benchmark and generated advertiser pages rather than live firm websites or economic outcome data.
Limitations / Open Gaps: The evaluation assumes a fixed candidate set and an advertiser page already admitted into retrieval results, so it optimizes citation likelihood conditional on retrieval rather than full end-to-end search visibility. The setup uses a controlled RAG-style pipeline rather than live proprietary systems such as ChatGPT, Perplexity, Gemini, or Google AI Overviews. Content quality is evaluated with LLM judges, which may not fully match human judgments. The evolutionary optimization requires repeated LLM calls, creating computational cost and reproducibility challenges. The paper does not directly measure clicks, traffic, conversions, firm performance, or other economic outcomes.
Verification Status: Partially verified: arXiv page checked; GitHub repository exists and contains code/requirements/quick-start instructions; GitHub README states accepted to ACL 2026, but official ACL proceedings/listing should be checked later; dataset availability refers to the public GEO-Bench benchmark rather than a new standalone dataset from this paper.
Notes: This paper should be treated as the fourth item in the GEO literature sequence: 1_GEO introduces the core GEO concept, 2_SAGEO Arena provides a full-pipeline benchmark, 3_Citation Absorption separates citation selection from answer absorption, and 4_Think Before Writing proposes a feature-level optimization method for improving citation visibility. It is especially useful for the memo section on website adaptation and controllable content features, but its finance/econ priority should be Medium-High rather than Must Read because it is primarily a technical optimization paper.

Paper Title: AgenticGEO: A Self-Evolving Agentic System for Generative Engine Optimization
Authors: Jiaqi Yuan; Jialu Wang; Zihan Wang; Qingyun Sun; Ruijie Wang; Jianxin Li
Venue / Status: arXiv preprint; ACM-style template contains venue placeholders; code and model are publicly available
Topic Category: Generative Engine Optimization; AI search visibility; agentic systems; black-box optimization; content-conditioned rewriting; domain generalization
Paper Type: Method paper + empirical evaluation + agentic optimization framework
Main Research Question: How can GEO systems adapt rewriting strategies to heterogeneous source content and non-stationary black-box generative engines while reducing the need for costly direct engine feedback?
Key Findings: AgenticGEO reframes GEO as a content-conditioned control problem and replaces fixed rewrite heuristics with a self-evolving MAP-Elites strategy archive plus a co-evolving critic. Static heuristic strategies are shown to be strategy- and content-sensitive, with many instances difficult to optimize. Across GEO-Bench, MS MARCO, and E-commerce, AgenticGEO outperforms 14 static and learning-based baselines on two downstream generative engines. The critic reduces reliance on expensive GE feedback, preserving most performance under sparse feedback, while semantic consistency analysis suggests that gains do not simply come from aggressive content drift.
Data Source(s): GEO-Bench for in-domain training/evaluation; MS MARCO for out-of-domain transfer; custom E-commerce dataset sourced from Amazon product descriptions and reviews. Reported preprocessed statistics: GEO-Bench 1,000 queries / 5,000 docs; MS MARCO 1,000 queries / 5,000 docs; E-commerce 416 queries / 2,180 docs.
AI Systems / Platforms Studied: Downstream generative engines: Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct and Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct. System components: Qwen2.5-1.5B critic, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct evolver, and Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct rewriter.
Outcome Variables: Attributed Word Count; Position-Weighted Citation Order; Overall impression score; cross-domain overall score; critic ranking quality with NDCG@K; GE feedback efficiency; semantic similarity / BERTScore-F1 between original and rewritten content.
Methods: Offline critic preference alignment using strategy-context preference pairs; online strategy-critic co-evolution with a MAP-Elites quality-diversity archive; value-novelty archive updates; sibling-aware advantage-weighted regression for the evolver; LoRA fine-tuning of critic and evolver; critic-guided multi-turn rewriting at inference; in-domain, cross-domain, ablation, feedback-budget, and semantic-consistency evaluations.
Dataset Available?: Yes — uses public GEO-Bench and MS MARCO; repository includes MSdata; custom E-commerce dataset is used and should be verified in the repository before treating it as fully reusable.
Relevance for Finance/Econ Project: Medium-high relevance. The paper is useful for understanding how firms, advertisers, or content providers might dynamically adapt webpage text to improve visibility and attribution in AI-mediated search systems. Its E-commerce setting and content-conditioned strategy selection are especially relevant for studying strategic website optimization, advertising visibility, platform intermediation, and firm exposure under changing AI search behavior. However, it does not directly measure firm traffic, revenue, conversions, market outcomes, or live commercial-platform behavior.
Limitations / Open Gaps: The study is a controlled benchmark evaluation rather than a live audit of ChatGPT, Perplexity, Gemini, or Google AI Overviews. It assumes the creator document is already in the candidate set and optimizes visibility/attribution in generated outputs rather than modeling the full upstream retrieval and ranking process. Experiments rely on open-source LLM-based engines and automatic impression metrics, so transfer to proprietary systems is uncertain. The approach requires substantial compute and repeated GE/LLM feedback. The paper evaluates semantic similarity but not human trust, user clicks, conversion, firm outcomes, or welfare effects. The ACM-style venue fields remain placeholders in the current arXiv/PDF version.
Verification Status: Verified: arXiv/PDF checked; GitHub repository exists with code, scripts, model-related assets, and MSdata folder. Publication venue is not verified beyond arXiv because the current PDF contains ACM template placeholders.
Notes: Strong technical GEO method paper. Best used after FeatGEO as an example of adaptive, agentic, self-evolving optimization: instead of selecting one fixed GEO rewrite, the system learns which rewriting strategies work for which content and reduces expensive black-box engine feedback through a critic. For the finance/econ memo, frame it as a method for strategic content adaptation rather than as evidence of real firm-level economic effects.

Paper Title: Structural Feature Engineering for Generative Engine Optimization: How Content Structure Shapes Citation Behavior
Authors: Junwei Yu; Mufeng Yang; Yepeng Ding; Hiroyuki Sato
Venue / Status: arXiv preprint; no top-conference venue status found in the paper or arXiv record as of review
Institution / Affiliation: The University of Tokyo; University of Tsukuba; Hiroshima University; The University of Tokyo / National Institute of Informatics
Topic Category: Generative Engine Optimization; structural content optimization; AI search visibility; citation behavior; structured content; schema-like/machine-readable website design; feature engineering
Main Research Question: How do webpage structural features, independent of semantic content changes, shape citation behavior in generative engines, and can structure-only optimization improve citation rate and visibility while preserving semantic integrity?
Key Findings: The paper proposes GEO-SFE, a structural feature engineering framework that decomposes content into macro-structure, meso-structure, and micro-structure. It reports that structural optimization improves citation performance by 17.3% on average and improves subjective perceptual quality by 18.5%. Ablation suggests that macro-structure contributes the largest share of the gains, followed by meso-structure and then micro-structure. The paper argues that heading hierarchy, paragraph chunking, lists/tables, internal linking, and visual emphasis can influence how generative engines parse and cite content, making structure a complementary optimization target to semantic GEO.
Data Source(s): Evaluation dataset constructed from GEO-Bench: 200 sampled articles across six domains (Biography, Health, Technology, Finance, Travel, Science), paired with 377 real-world queries. The paper reports an average article length of 2,547 words and evaluates original versus structurally optimized versions across six generative-engine platforms, producing 2,400 test cases.
AI Systems / Platforms Studied: Google SGE; Bing Chat; Perplexity AI; Phind; ChatGPT; Claude. The paper groups them into Search-then-Synthesize, Iterative Refinement, and Integrated Search-Generation architectures.
Outcome Variables: Citation Rate; Visibility Score combining coverage, position, and influence; Citation Depth; First Position; G-Eval subjective metrics including relevance, influence, uniqueness, subjective position, subjective count, click probability, and diversity.
Methods: The paper formalizes generative-engine citation visibility as a function of coverage, position, and influence, then represents page structure through macro-, meso-, and micro-level feature vectors. It proposes hierarchical feature extraction, architecture-specific citation prediction using gradient boosting-style models, and structural optimization algorithms for document architecture, paragraph/list/table chunking, and sentence-level emphasis while applying semantic preservation constraints. Evaluation compares baseline and optimized content across six platforms, uses paired significance tests, and conducts ablation by removing macro-, meso-, and micro-structure optimization.
Dataset Available?: Not available yet — the paper uses a GEO-Bench sample, but the exact sampled evaluation set, transformations, and reproduction package are not linked.
Relevance for Finance/Econ Project: Medium-high relevance. This paper is directly useful for the finance/econ project because it focuses on how firms or websites may adapt webpage structure, headings, chunking, lists/tables, internal links, and visual emphasis to improve visibility in AI-generated answers. It is especially relevant to research questions about machine-readable websites, structured content, disclosure architecture, investor-relations pages, product pages, and strategic adaptation to AI-mediated search. The inclusion of a Finance domain also makes the connection clearer, although the paper does not measure firm-level economic outcomes.
Limitations / Open Gaps: No public author-provided code or standalone dataset link was found. The exact sampled dataset, prompts, structural transformations, platform versions, and timing of platform queries are not fully reproducible from the paper alone. The six generative-engine evaluation may be sensitive to live platform changes. Subjective quality relies on G-Eval with Gemini 2.5 Pro. Several references are industry/blog sources, and the reported 17.3% citation improvement should be treated as needing independent replication. The paper does not study actual firm behavior, web traffic, revenue, conversion, or market outcomes.
Verification Status: Partially verified: arXiv and uploaded PDF checked; no official author-provided GitHub/code link or standalone dataset link found; a third-party implementation was found online but was not counted as paper code; quantitative claims should be treated cautiously until reproducible code/data are released.
PDF File Name: 6_Structural Feature Engineering for Generative Engine Optimization.pdf
Notes: Useful paper for the structured-content/schema side of the literature review. Best used as supporting evidence that GEO is not only about rewriting wording but also about page architecture and information structure. Because reproducibility information is limited, cite cautiously and do not treat it as strongly verified as papers with public code and data.
